coredata.dc:title: Excitation-independent and fluorescence-reversible N-GQD for picomolar detection of inhibitory neurotransmitter in milk samples ‒ an alleyway for possible neuromorphic computing application
coredata.dc:creator: Sravya, V.
Pavithra, V.R.
Thangadurai, T. Daniel
Nataraj, D.
Kumar, N. Sathish
coredata.dc:description:
N‒GQDs with an average size of ca. 20–30 nm are utilized for the picomolar detection of inhibitory neurotransmitters, glycine (Gly), in pH ca. 7.0. The crystalline nature, morphology, elemental composition, and chemical state of N–GQDs are investigated by XRD, FE-SEM, HR-TEM, XPS, and FT-IR techniques. The addition of Gly (100 × 10−9 M; 0 → 1.0 mL) steadily quenches the fluorescence intensity of N–GQD (1 × 10−6 M) at 432 nm (λ ex 333 nm) due to inner filter effect (IFE) through the formation of ground-state complex, N–GQD•Gly. The excitation-independent N‒GQDs showed an outstanding selectivity and sensitivity towards Gly with binding constant (Ka = 8.97 × 10−3 M−1) and LoD (21.04 pM; S/N = 3). Time-correlated single-photon counting experiment confirms the static quenching of N–GQD (8.77 → 8.85 ns) in the presence of Gly. The interference of other amino acids on the strong binding of the N–GQD•Gly complex in H2O is examined. Combinatorial Ex-OR and NOT gate logic circuits that could be useful in neuromorphic computing are developed based on the reversible fluorescence intensity changes of N–GQD upon the addition of Gly (Ф
F 0.54 → 0.39). The real-time application of N–GQD was investigated using commercially available relevant milk samples. Remarkably, not less than 99% cytotoxic reactivity of N–GQDs is attained against HeLa cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N‒GQD
Glycine
Logic gate
Neuromorphic computing
Cytotoxic
Milk samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Author VS sincerely expresses gratitude to Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore, for the research facilities. Authors VRP and TDT gratefully acknowledge SNR Charitable Trust, Coimbatore, for the financial support through Institutional Research Development Fellowship and providing XRD and DST‒FIST facilities, and Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, New Delhi. We sincerely thank Dr. P. Moorthi, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College – Coimbatore, and Mr. Manikandan, Bharathiar University – Coimbatore; for their constant support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction profile of GQD (a) and N–GQD (b).
HR–TEM images of N–GQD. Scale bar (a → f) 100, 50, 20, 10, 5 and 2 nm, respectively; (inset e) SAED pattern.
XPS of N–GQD: survey spectrum (a); high-resolution XPS of C 1s (b); N 1s (c); O 1s (d). XPS spectrum of GQD can be found in Fig. SI2.

Changes in the fluorescence emission intensity changes of N-GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) upon addition of amino acids (10.0 × 10−6 M) in H2O (slit width 5 nm; λ ex 333 nm). (Arg-arginine, Asp-aspargine, Cys-cysteine, Gly-glycine, Glu-glutamic acid, His-histidine, Isol-isoleucine, Leu-leucine, Lys-lysine, Val-valine, Trp-tryptophan, and Met-methionine).
Representation of IFE (a), and ground-state complex formation between Gly and N–GQD (b). [N‒GQD] = 1.0 × 10−6 M; [Gly] = 100.0 × 10−9 M; (N‒GQD: λ ex 333 nm; Gly: λ ex 273 nm; slit width 5 nm).

(a) Changes in the fluorescence spectrum of N‒GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) upon addition of Gly (100.0 × 10−9 M; 0 → 1.0 mL) in H2O (λ ex 333 nm; slit width 5 nm). (inset) Corresponding reaction solution under daylight and UV-lamp (λ ex 365 nm); (b) Corresponding linear fit. Final concentrations of N‒GQD and Gly after the titration are 0.666 × 10−6 M and 33.3 × 10−9 M, respectively.

(a) Changes in the absorption spectrum of N‒GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) upon addition of Gly (100 × 10−9 M; 0 → 1.0 mL) in H2O. (b) Corresponding linear fit. Final concentrations of N‒GQD and Gly after the titration are 0.666 × 10−6 M and 33.3 × 10−9 M, respectively.
Time-resolved fluorescence decay of N‒GQDs (1.0 × 10−6 M) in the absence and presence of Gly (1.0, 2.5, 5.0 and 10.0 eq.); (λ ex 333 nm; emission monochromator 338 nm).

(a) 3D representation of changes in the fluorescence intensity of N‒GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) upon addition of Gly (100 × 10−9 M) in presence and absence of EDTA (100 × 10−9 M) in H2O (λ ex 333 nm; slit width 5.0 nm). ① N‒GQD, ② N‒GQD + Gly, ③ N‒GQD + Gly + EDTA, and ④ N‒GQD + Gly + EDTA + Gly. Corresponding fluorescence spectrum can be found in Fig. SI12; (b) Corresponding reaction solution under UV-lamp (λ ex 365 nm); (c) Applicable truth table for the developed logic circuit; (d) Schematic diagram of a developed combinatorial logic gate.

Changes in the fluorescence spectrum of N‒GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) upon addition of (a) cow milk spiked Gly (100.0 × 10−9 M; 0 → 1.0 mL), and (b) soya milk spiked Gly (100.0 × 10−9 M; 0 → 1.0 mL) in H2O (λ ex 333 nm; slit width 5 nm). (insets) Corresponding linear fit; Final concentrations of N‒GQD and milk samples spiked Gly after the titration are 0.666 × 10−6 M and 33.33 × 10−9 M, respectively.
Determination of Gly in milk samples by N-GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M; 2.0 mL).

row.entry: Initial conc. of Gly before titration (nM)
Final conc. of Gly after titration (nM)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
#
λ ex: 332 nm; spiked and non-spiked Gly sample volume added: 0 → 1.0 mL; $ Relative Standard Deviation.

head.ce:title: Excitation-independent and fluorescence-reversible N-GQD for picomolar detection of inhibitory neurotransmitter in milk samples ‒ an alleyway for possible neuromorphic computing application

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors have equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
N‒GQDs with an average size of ca. 20–30 nm are utilized for the picomolar detection of inhibitory neurotransmitters, glycine (Gly), in pH ca. 7.0. The crystalline nature, morphology, elemental composition, and chemical state of N–GQDs are investigated by XRD, FE-SEM, HR-TEM, XPS, and FT-IR techniques. The addition of Gly (100 × 10−9 M; 0 → 1.0 mL) steadily quenches the fluorescence intensity of N–GQD (1 × 10−6 M) at 432 nm (λ ex 333 nm) due to inner filter effect (IFE) through the formation of ground-state complex, N–GQD•Gly. The excitation-independent N‒GQDs showed an outstanding selectivity and sensitivity towards Gly with binding constant (Ka = 8.97 × 10−3 M−1) and LoD (21.04 pM; S/N = 3). Time-correlated single-photon counting experiment confirms the static quenching of N–GQD (8.77 → 8.85 ns) in the presence of Gly. The interference of other amino acids on the strong binding of the N–GQD•Gly complex in H2O is examined. Combinatorial Ex-OR and NOT gate logic circuits that could be useful in neuromorphic computing are developed based on the reversible fluorescence intensity changes of N–GQD upon the addition of Gly (Ф
F 0.54 → 0.39). The real-time application of N–GQD was investigated using commercially available relevant milk samples. Remarkably, not less than 99% cytotoxic reactivity of N–GQDs is attained against HeLa cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Glycine (Gly), a small, simple, and most essential amino acid with no D or L configuration play a vital role in the peripheral and central nervous systems (CNS) [1]. In addition to its renowned roles as (i) a conjugator of low-molecular-weight toxic substances, and (ii) an inhibitory neurotransmitter [2], it has been reported recently that Gly can improve the sleeping quality in humans by decreasing core body temperature (CBT) [3]. The standard Gly concentration in plasma is 0.25–0.50 mM, which corresponds to a normal intake of 1.50–3.00 g/day [4]. The good sources of Gly are meat and meat products, grain products, milk and dairy products, vegetables, and seafood. Though a trifling dearth of Gly is not that much harmful to health, a vast deficiency may lead to failure of the immune response, abnormal nutrient metabolism, digestion, brain and cognitive functioning, low growth, etc [5]. Fascinatingly, it has been demonstrated that even high daily doses of Gly are safe and may well also be useful in the prevention or treatment of metabolic syndrome [6]. Based on the above facts, Gly can be considered as an important amino acid for human health, and to monitor its level, developing a cost-effective, swift and very sensitive analytical method is extremely essential.

There are a few materials and methods such as ion-pair high-performance liquid chromatography (IP-HPLC) [7], Raman spectroscopy [8], electrochemical [9,10], and high-performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence (HPLC-Fluo) [11] that are being utilized for the determination of Gly through different mechanisms. Excitingly, a nanocomposite material has also been utilized for the sensitive detection of Gly through the electrochemical method [12]. Besides, a computational design methodology is also employed to develop a FRET-based optical sensor to monitor extracellular Gly levels [13]. Unfortunately, these materials and methods have a lot of drawbacks such as difficulty in sample preparation, expense, repeatability, etc. Nevertheless, owing to its comfort, sensitivity, inexpensive, immediate response, and proficiency of recognition of specific analyte, fluorescence technique is regarded as one of the superlative methods to detect an analyte of interest instantly [14,15]. Because of that, for human health, a researcher has to prudently design an anatomically modified material that may well enhance the rapid determination of very low concentrations of Gly for real-time application.

Recently, carbon-based nanostructured materials gained prominence owing to their distinctive properties, stability, etc. [16,17]. Among the carbon-based nanostructured materials reported in the literature, graphene quantum dots (GQD) have progressed very strongly in industry, healthcare, and energy-related applications [18–21] due to their superior optoelectronic, electrical, and catalytic properties [18,22]. Though GQD exhibits superior properties, it still possesses disadvantages in its photophysical properties which pacifies its practical applications [23]. To overcome all the aforementioned photophysical problems, the functionalization or doping of GQD by a fluorophore moiety or heteroatom could be an available notion, which can also make the detection process easier [24,25]. Even though various heteroatom doped GQDs, for example, N–GQD, P–GQD, B-GQD, and S-GQD, have been utilized for numerous applications [26–29], an exhaustive literature survey reveals that only a few reports are available for N–GQD sensing applications, but none of them has reported Gly detection [30–33]. Though glycine is a non-essential amino acid, maintaining the balanced concentration in the daily supplement is much important as being a neurotransmitter. Herein, we report the determination of glycine in commercial sources and real samples. Milk is an important dairy source and daily source of glycine, which is easily available and can be estimated without tedious sample preparation techniques. We use a fluorescence spectrophotometer to investigate the concentration of glycine present in aqueous sand different milk sources using the N–GQDs.

In continuation of our recent attention to carbon-based quantum dot applications [24,25,34,35], herein, we report N–GQD with an average size of ca. 20–30 nm for simple, rapid, selective, and ultra-low detection of an inhibitory neurotransmitter, Gly, with a very low detection limit (LoD) as ca. 21 pM in pH ca.7.0 through inner filter effect (IFE). Fluorescence decay profile and interference studies are carried out and reported. Based on the reversible fluorescence intensity nature of N–GQD upon addition of Gly, a combinatorial logic circuit, three-input Ex-OR, and dual-input NOT gate, has been developed which may be a useful instrument in neuromorphic computing to study the biological functions of the neurotransmitter, Gly. The achieved real-time application data of N–GQD using commercially available milk samples are in accordance with commercial Gly experimental results. Additionally, cytotoxicity and cell viability of N–GQDs to HeLa cells are also examined as per ISO 10993:5 certification.

A simple and fast method is employed to synthesize N−GQDs based on direct heating of citric acid (CA) and urea. In a typical experimental procedure, 2.0 g of CA and 0.5 g of urea are taken in a 50 mL beaker and heated for ca. 200 °C. After 30 min, 20 mL of deionized water is added drop by drop under vigorous stirring to obtain surfactant-free N−GQDs for characterization and further application.

All the reagents and solvents involved in the synthesis are analytical grade and used without any further purification. Double distilled water is used throughout the experiment. Chemicals such as CA, urea, amino acids, and solvents are purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India, TCI Chemicals, and HiMedia.

Infrared spectra are recorded on a Bruker ATR-FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Alpha-T, Germany). X-ray diffraction measurements are carried out using an x-ray source with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.542 Å) (X'Pert powder diffractometer, PANalytical, The Netherlands). The surface morphology is analyzed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (200 kV FETEM, model JEM-2100F, JEOL) at CUSAT, Kochi, India, and FESEM (SIGMA HV-Carl Zeiss with Bruker Quantax 200-Z10 EDS Detector) at Coimbatore Institute of Technology, Coimbatore, India. UV–Visible absorption is carried out on an Analytik Jena spectrophotometer (Model SZ100, Germany). Fluorescence experiments are recorded on an Agilent Technologies spectrofluorophotometer (G9800A) at 298 K. Raman spectra are recorded on Horiba Labram HR with a 532 nm laser at Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India. The PL lifetime measurements are carried out using an IBH time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy is recorded on Thermo Scientific (MULTILAB 2000 Base system with X-ray, Auger, and ISS) with Twin Anode Mg/Al (300/400 W) X-ray source at CSIR-CECRI, Karaikudi, India. Cytotoxic reactivity studies are carried out at SITRA, Coimbatore, India.

Commercial milk samples such as cows and soya are purchased from nearby shops. To reduce the matrix effect, the milk samples are diluted 50-fold with deionized water (Milli Q, Millipore, Barnstead, CA, USA) without any treatment. The standard milk samples are spiked with a known concentration of Gly (200.0 × 10−9 M). The fluorescence emission intensity changes of N–GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) are monitored upon the addition of spiked milk samples with Gly concentration as 100.0 × 10−9 M. The percentage of recovery of Gly is calculated by using the following Eq. (1).
(1)
R e c o v e r y
% =
C o n c .
o f
s p i k e d
s a m p l e − C o n c .
o f
n o n − s p i k e d
s a m p l e
S p i k i n g
C o n c e n t r a t i o n
× 100

A molecular computer is an important apparatus to explore the biological functions of an important analyte by mimicking the basic principle of silicon chips [36]. The main objective of the current research is to provide a possible contribution to the development of the neuromorphic computing system [37,38] for the powerful inhibitory neurotransmitter, Gly, in the future. On the subject of the exceptional physicochemical properties, GQD can be a good candidate to consider for the aforesaid objective. Thus, a researcher has to design and synthesize the proper material to overwhelm all the reported photophysical problems of GQDs so for. The surface functionalization or doping of heteroatom could be an effective approach that could lead the researchers to a variety of applications [39–41]. In the current study, incomplete carbonization of equimolar citric acid (CA) and urea yielded N–GQD in a spherical shape without the addition of any surfactant (Scheme SI1). The as-prepared N–GQD is characterized by standard physiochemical methods to extend its application further towards the molecular computer domain.

The crystalline nature of N–GQD is examined by the XRD technique (Fig. 1 ). The broad 2θ diffraction peak centered ca. at 17.93° (Fig. 1b) corresponds to (002) plane of graphitic lattice space of highly disordered N–GQD [42], is due to the presence of more number of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as –OH, –COOH, and –CONH in N–GQD, which cause a paucity in substantial order [43]. The calculated interlayer d spacing for GQD and N–GQD is found to be ca. 3.76 and 5.09 Å, respectively, which is in good harmony with the literature values of GQD (3.43–4.85 Å) [44]. This increase in interlayer spacing (3.76 → 5.09 Å) can be attributed to the introduction of nitrogen between the layers [45]. Furthermore, the presence of nitrogen in N–GQD generates internal strain and thus shifts the 2θ toward a lower value (23.65° → 17.93°) and decreases the peak intensity (123 → 37 a. u.).

Electron microscopy techniques such as FE–SEM, and HR–TEM is utilized to analyze the surface morphology of the N–GQD. The FE–SEM images indicate that N–GQD is spherical with an average diameter of ca. 20–30 nm (Fig. SI1). HR–TEM images of N–GQD in diverse magnifications provide evidence for the formation of quantum dots with an average diameter of ca. 20–30 nm (Fig. 2 ). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern illustrates the single-crystalline nature of the N–GQD with the Debye–Scherer diffraction corresponding to (002) graphitic plane (Fig. 2e inset). High magnification images of N–GQD confirm the nano dimension with lattice fringes spacing of ca. 0.25 nm for the (002) plane (Fig. 2d–f).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy survey spectrum of N–GQD confirms the existence of C 1s at 283.38 eV, N 1s at 398.75 eV, and O 1s at 530.82 eV and gives C, N, and O contents of 78.74, 6.39, and 14.87%, respectively (Fig. 3 a and Table SI1). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum indicates that the N–GQD is more graphitic-like, with distinct –C–C−/−C–H, –CN−, and –O–CC− peaks at 283.13, 284.18, and 286.79 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b). From the high-resolution N 1s spectrum, a sharp peak is observed at 398.55 eV, corresponding to –CN, which indicates that the nature of nitrogen attached to the GQD is –CN category (Fig. 3c). The lower atomic percent ratio of nitrogen-to-carbon (0.0812) suggests that nitrogen molecules are chemically bonded to the GQD in a monodentate manner [45]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 530.48 and 532.05 eV, which are attributed to the O–C− and –C–OH, respectively (Fig. 3d). The low oxygen-to-carbon ratio (0.1888) agrees with high-quality graphene structures prepared by previously reported procedures [42]. These XPS results further reveal that doping of nitrogen in GQD with urea moiety is achievable.

Unfortunately, Raman bands for N–GQD is not observed even in different experimental conditions such as (i) diverse laser sources (785 and 532 nm), (ii) high and low energy, (iii) different temperatures (60–70 °C and 150 °C), and (iv) coating on different substrates (glass and silicon), owing to the highly fluorescent and reactive nature of N−GQDs [46].

FTIR spectrum of N−GQD corroborates the (i) presence of oxygen-containing functional groups such as –OH and –COOH (3618, 3242, 1712, and 1089 cm−1), (ii) incomplete carbonization of CA (2928, 1633, 1415, and 684 cm−1), and (iii) formation of new bond analogous to –CN (3323 and 1228 cm−1) upon functionalization (Fig. SI3). The UV–visible absorption spectra of N–GQD exhibit noticeable blue shifted (ca. 20 nm) strong absorption peak ca. at 272 nm (π→π*) compared with GQD (292 nm) and a broad shoulder peak ca. at 333 nm (n→π*) (Fig. SI4). The fluorescence spectrum of N–GQD displays a slightly blue-shifted (ca. 13 nm) sharp intense peak (432 nm; λ ex 333 nm) compared with GQD (445 nm; λ ex 378 nm) (Fig. SI5). The excitation dependency of N–GQD is examined at a different wavelength (300 → 380 nm). Upon different wavelength of excitations, the emission wavelength of N–GQD (λ em 432 nm) is not shifted aside from the intensity, which gradually increased up to the maximum excitation (λex330 nm) and started to decrease further (Fig. SI6) [47] which is perhaps due to various reasons such as size, quantum confinement, etc [48].

To extend the applications of N–GQD further in the direction of molecular computing, the selective amino acid sensing property of N–GQD is investigated by fluorescence technique in H2O. Fig. 4 illustrates the fluorescence emission intensity changes of N-GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) upon addition of amino acids (10.0 × 10−6 M) such as Arginine (Arg), Aspargine (Asp), Cysteine (Cys), Glutamic Acid (Glu), H-histidine (His), I-isoleucine (Isol), Leucine (Leu), Lysine (Lys), Valine (Val), Tryptophan (Trp), and Methionine (Met) in H2O (slit width 5 nm; λ ex 333 nm). Interestingly, a ca. 5-fold decrease in the fluorescence emission intensity of N-GQD is observed only upon the addition of Gly. The addition of other amino acids did not yield any noticeable transformations in the fluorescence emission intensity of N-GQD which ratifies the selectivity of N–GQD towards Gly in physiological conditions.

Based on the selectivity result, the sensitivity of N–GQD towards Gly is measured by adding different concentrations of Gly (100.0 × 10−9 M; 0 → 1.0 mL) to an aqueous solution of N–GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) (Fig. 6). The gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity of N–GQD at 432 nm upon increasing the concentration of Gly could be due to the inner filter effect (IFE) (Fig. 5 a) through the formation of ground-state complex, N–GQD•Gly (Fig. 5b) [34]. Fig. 5a clearly illustrates the complete overlapping of the absorption and that the emission spectrum of Gly with the absorption and emission spectrum of N–GQD causes the decrease in fluorescence intensity of N–GQD at 432 nm. The calculated binding constant (Ka = 8.97 × 10−3 M−1, SI eq (1)), LoD (21.04 × 10−12 M, SI eq (2)) (Fig. SI7), and quantum yield of fluorescence (Ф
F 0.536 → 0.396) suggest that N–GQD could be an operative fluorescent sensor for the inhibitory neurotransmitter, Gly, with a very low detection limit in H2O.

To highlight the currently achieved fluorescence results, we have carried out UV–visible absorption titration studies under similar experimental conditions (Fig. 7 ). Both the absorption bands of N–GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) at 272 and 333 nm gradually decrease upon the addition of Gly (100.0 × 10−9 M; 0 → 1.0 mL). This steady decrease in absorption intensity is owing to the formation of the ground-state complex, N–GQD•Gly, through static interaction between N–GQD and Gly. From the UV–visible absorption studies, the quenching rate constant (Kq = 1.867 × 105 LM−1S−1), apparent association constant (Kapp = 1.75 × 10−2 M−1), and LoD (40.8 × 10−12 M) (Fig. SI8–SI10) are calculated by using the Stern-Volmer equation (SI eq (3)), Benesi-Hildebrand method (SI eq (4)), and standard equation (SI eq (2)), respectively, which is consistent with the fluorescence data.

To confirm the fluorescence quenching of N–GQD upon the addition of Gly, a time-correlated single-photon counting experiment (TCSPC) is carried out for N–GQD with and without Gly. TCSPC experiment data for free N–GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) is best fitted with tri-exponential function to yield an average lifetime of 8.77 ns (Fig. 8 ; Table SI2), slightly higher than GQD (6.60 ns) [24], which confirms the presence of free N–GQD, interacting N–GQD, and a supplementary element. The equation used to calculate the lifetime of the materials is given in Table SI2. The addition of 10.0 eq. of Gly did not significantly increase the lifetime value of N–GQD (8.85 ns) and changed the functionality as well. Due to the addition of a higher concentration of the analyte, the collision between the fluorophore and the analyte increases which decreases the fluorescence intensity as a results fluorescence lifetime decreases. Another reason is quencher diffusion which occurs at higher concentrations is also responsible for the decrease in lifetime value. However, an increase in concentration decreases the fluorescence quantum yield which in turn results in a very short increase in a lifetime [49,50]. This very diminutive increase in the average lifetime explains the formation of a ground-state complex between N–GQD and Gly and as a result, static quenching occurs [25,34,49,50]. During the static quenching process, the added analyte, Gly, which is small in size (topological polar surface area 63.3 Å2), may surround the N–GQD domain. As a result, it averts the photons to reach N–GQD. More importantly, the rate of the reaction, i.e. the quenching rate, is slow even in a higher concentration of Gly (10.0 eq) which causes a very small increase in the average lifetime value (8.77 → 8.85 ns) for N‒GQD•Gly complex. In higher concentrations, the quencher and the fluorophore are closer to each other. As a result rapid quenching occurs, followed by a slower quenching rate due to the quencher diffusion [49]. An additional reason for this tiny increase in the average fluorescence lifetime of N–GQD after the addition of Gly proposes that the quenching process is probably due to the IFE [51] which is demonstrated through Fig. 5a. Predictably, the same pattern of fluorescence lifetime profile result (average lifetime value 7.89 → 8.05 ns) is observed for N–GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) in the presence of less concentration of Gly (0.025, 0.05, and 0.10) under similar experimental conditions (Fig. SI11 and Table SI3) which further supports the static quenching and IFE mechanism. The observed CHISQ values in both lower (0.025, 0.05, and 0.1) and higher equivalents quotient (1.0, 2.5, 5.0, and 10.0) are significant and best meaningful fit with tri-exponential decay which lies between 0.8 and 1.4 [52].

To ensure a strong interaction between N–GQD and Gly, competing experiments are carried out in the presence of various amino acids (1.0 × 10−9 M) by using the fluorescence technique. The complex amino acids to the quantity of 10 eq. each including Alanine (Ala), Arginine (Arg), Aspargine (Asp), Cysteine (Cys), Glutamic Acid (Glu), H-histidine (His), I-isoleucine (Isol), Leucine (Leu), Lysine (Lys), Valine (Val), Tryptophan (Trp), and Methionine (Met) are added into the aqueous solution of N‒GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) with Gly (0.1 × 10−9 M), respectively. No discernible fluorescence emission intensity changes are observed in the presence of intrusive amino acids, which emphasizes the fact that the augmenting selectivity of N‒GQD towards Gly is practically not influenced by other amino acids (Fig. SI12). This observation conspicuously validates the high selectivity and anti-interference sensing aptitude of N‒GQD towards the powerful inhibitory neurotransmitter, Gly, in the rivalry environment. To analyze the selectivity and interference effect of quencher metal ions on the complex N‒GQD•Gly, we carried out additional experiments with chloride salts of Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, and Hg2+ under similar experimental conditions. Fig. SI13 describes that there is no change in the fluorescence emission intensity of the complex N‒GQD•Gly other than the dilution effect.

Based on the aforesaid interesting spectrophotometric results and cogitation of extending N‒GQD application further towards molecular computing, giving an attempt to develop a logic gate circuit, for neuromorphic computing application, is praiseworthy. To make evident this objective, we performed fluorescence studies using Gly and EDTA at pH ca. 7.0. The reversible fluorescence nature of N‒GQD displayed in Fig. SI14 inspired us to develop a combinatorial molecular logic gate circuit using N‒GQD, Gly, and EDTA as inputs 1, 2, and 3, respectively, for the inhibitory neurotransmitter, Gly (Fig. 9 ). These three input variables are fed to a three-input Ex-OR gate to monitor fluorescence intensity changes of N‒GQD at 432 nm as an output R1 (Fig. 9d). Generally, the output fluorescence intensity could be considered as a Boolean value of either ‘1’ or ‘0’ [53]. The fluorescence intensity of N‒GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) immediately quenches upon the addition of Gly (100.0 × 10−9 M), i.e. the output is ‘1’. Addition of EDTA (100.0 × 10−9 M) to the aqueous solution of N‒GQD containing Gly further decreased the fluorescence intensity (output R1). Even so, subsequent addition of input Gly to output R1 resulted in output R2 (two-input Ex-OR gate), in which the original intensity of N‒GQD•Gly is nearly attained (output is ‘1’) which implies that complexation between Gly and N‒GQD is reversible (Fig. 9a and b). To obtain the desirable result (R3), the inverter gate, NOT, is applied at the output stage of R2. Consequently, a combinatorial logic circuit has been achieved (Fig. 9d) according to the truth table (Fig. 9c) [54]. It is worth mentioning that the currently developed combinatorial logic circuit is an infrequent logic gate system operated in physiological conditions (pH ca. 7.0) and cascading could be possible to generate a large digit password [55]. The fundamental building block of neuromorphic computing is a spiking neuron which is very similar to a logic gate [56]. The brain is the central processing unit that evaluates and processes the signals. Boolean log gates are cascaded to form higher-order adder circuits. When three inputs are considered the frequently used logic gate is 3 input Ex-OR gate. More gates are cascaded to achieve the desired Boolean function [57]. The overall fluorescence spectrum and the truth table are already presented in Fig. 9 (a). The basic operation of the 3 input Ex-OR gate is elaborated in the form of binary truth table logic is given in supporting information (Table SI4).

The examination of in vitro cytotoxicity of a sensing probe is very essential for its biological application, especially towards human health. To demonstrate this objective and to ensure one of the important superior properties of GQDs, i.e. biocompatibility and low toxicity [60], the cytotoxic effect of N–GQDs are investigated using HeLa cells with MTT viability assay (1 mg/mL in fetal bovine serum (FBS)). The HeLa cells are grown up in MEM medium complemented with 10% FBS and treated with different concentrations of N–GQDs (0.0, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0, 7.5, and 10.0 μM; 100 μL) for ca. 24 h at 37 ± 1 °C to demarcate the consequence of dose enslavement on incubation and concentration of the N–GQDs (Fig. SI17). Beguilingly, after the incubation period, the N–GQDs did not show any cytotoxic reactivity (“None” as per ISO 10993:5 standard chart; section SI5) even in high concentration (10.0 μM) (Fig. SI17b→f). This non-cytotoxic dose-dependency of N–GQDs on the biological activity of cell lines suggests that even high doses of N–GQDs cause no toxicity to HeLa cells as similar to the control (untreated; Fig. SI17a, SI eq (5)). No less than 99.0% cell viability for HeLa cells are perceived in a wide range of N–GQDs concentrations (from 1.0 to 10.0 μM) after ca. 24 h incubation (Fig. SI18, SI eq (6)) [61]. This aforesaid none cytotoxicity and 99% cell viability results authenticate the feasibility of N–GQDs as a prospective material for biomedical applications.

N‒GQD is a highly studied material for its various extraordinary luminescence properties and biocompatible nature. The current study is about how the N‒GQD undergoes IFE upon picomolar sensing of Gly in pH ca. 7.0 is addressed right now. The XRD data corroborates the graphitic nature of N–GQD. Microscopic images confirm the N–GQD morphology as spherical with an average diameter of ca. 20–30 nm. The selective and very sensitive Gly sensing property of N–GQD is due to the IFE through the formation of the ground-state complex, N–GQD•Gly. Additionally, fluorescence studies confirm that N–GQD is excitation independent (λ ex 300 → 380 nm) and it has reversible fluorescence emission property at 432 nm (λ ex 333 nm). The obtained LoD (ca. 21 pM) reveals that N–GQD can be quantitatively used to detect standard Gly at low concentration in plasma and is very low in comparison with already existing reports (Table SI5). Notably, the interferon study results validate that N–GQD had a very high selectivity towards Gly in physiological pH. The time-resolved fluorescence decay profile confirms the static quenching of N–GQD (1.0 × 10−6 M) in the presence of Gly (10 eq.) with a very little increase in the average lifetime (8.77 → 8.85 ns). The developed combinatorial molecular logic gate, three-input Ex-OR, and dual-input NOT gate, might serve as a suitable tool in neuromorphic computing to explore the biological functions of the neurotransmitter, Gly [62,63]. The relevant commercial milk sample analysis with a low detection limit (56.99 pM and 55.27 pM with Ф
F 0.54 → 0.240 and 0.54 → 0.238, respectively) also validates the application of N–GQD in clinical trials for the detection of a very low concentration of Gly in real-time. The obtained cytotoxic reactivity results of N–GQDs in the concentration range from 1.0 to 10.0 μM against HeLa cell lines suggest that N–GQDs can also be employed in the biological field for further applications such as bioimaging, biosensing, cellular in-vitro and in-vivo studies. Glycine detection in milk samples using N-GQDs enables a practicable application in near future to detect its concentration in disproportionate milk samples.

V. Sravya: Experiments and Characterization; V. R. Pavithra: Experiments and Data curation; T. Daniel THANGADURAI: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-Original draft, and Editing; D. Nataraj: Contribution to fluorescence decay profile studies; N. Sathish Kumar: Contribution to logic gate circuit development.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Author VS sincerely expresses gratitude to Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore, for the research facilities. Authors VRP and TDT gratefully acknowledge SNR Charitable Trust, Coimbatore, for the financial support through Institutional Research Development Fellowship and providing XRD and DST‒FIST facilities, and Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, New Delhi. We sincerely thank Dr. P. Moorthi, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College – Coimbatore, and Mr. Manikandan, Bharathiar University – Coimbatore; for their constant support.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

FE-SEM, XPS, FT-IR, Absorption and fluorescence studies, and LoD, quenching rate constant (Kq), apparent association constant (Kapp) calculation, and logic gate.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
